1.1: The letter opens with ho en ap arches — what was from the beginning — deliberately echoing John 1:1 (en arche en ho logos) while shifting from cosmological declaration to experiential testimony. The subject is not "in the beginning" as a time-point but "from the beginning" as a continuous reality now intersecting with apostolic history. The chain of verbs — heard, seen, looked at (etheasametha, the verb of prolonged beholding), handled (epselaphesen, physically touched) — builds from the most remote sense to the most intimate, asserting the full material reality of the incarnation against any spiritualizing tendency.
1.7: haima (haima) — blood functions here as covenant-cleansing agent in the Levitical sense: not merely moral example or martyrdom but the substance that purifies from sin. katharizei (katharizei) is present tense — ongoing, continuous cleansing, not once-accomplished and finished. apo pases hamartias — from every sin — the scope is universal and unqualified. The phrase haima Iesou tou huiou autou is theologically dense: Jesus (the human name) is the Son (divine relation), making the blood both fully human (real sacrifice) and of infinite worth (divine person).
1.9: pistos kai dikaios — faithful and just — pairs two divine attributes as the theological ground for forgiveness. Faithfulness (pistos) is covenant-fidelity to the promise of forgiveness; justice (dikaios) is the legal-moral attribute that might seem to demand punishment but here grounds forgiveness because the atonement has satisfied justice. God forgives justly — not by overlooking sin but because Christ has borne its penalty. afeisin (aphiemi) = to release, cancel; katharize = to purify, cleanse — two complementary images: legal cancellation and cultic purification.
1.2:
ἐφανερώθη (ephanerōthē) — aorist passive of φανερόω, "was manifested." The verb appears twice in this verse, bookending the parenthetical testimony. It is a key term in 1 John (used again at 3:2,5,8; 4:9) and signals that what was hidden has been disclosed — not that something new came into being, but that what already existed became publicly visible.

τὴν ζωὴν τὴν αἰώνιον — "the eternal life" (double article, emphatic). The definite article marks this not as a quality or concept but as a specific, identifiable reality that "was with the Father." The phrase πρὸς τὸν πατέρα ("with the Father") uses the same preposition as John 1:1 ("with God"), suggesting personal presence and relation rather than mere existence.
1.3:
κοινωνία (koinōnia, G2842) — rendered "fellowship" in the MKT, but the Greek root carries the sense of active sharing, partnership, and participation — more than social togetherness. The double occurrence ("fellowship with us... our fellowship is with the Father") maps the vertical and horizontal together: participation in the apostolic witness is simultaneously participation in the Father–Son relation.

The purpose clause ἵνα καὶ ὑμεῖς κοινωνίαν ἔχητε μεθ᾽ ἡμῶν — "so that you also may have fellowship with us" — places the Johannine community as the mediating link. The reader cannot bypass the apostolic witness to reach the divine fellowship directly.
1.4: χαρά (chara) — "joy." The pronoun is contested: some manuscripts read ἡμῶν ("our joy") and others ὑμῶν ("your joy"). The MKT follows "our joy" — the community whose proclamation and whose readers' reception together complete the joy of the eyewitnesses. πεπληρωμένη ᾖ — present perfect passive subjunctive: "may be (and remain) made full." Completeness of joy is the stated goal of writing, placing the letter's purpose within the tradition of Johannine joy language (cf. John 15:11; 16:24; 17:13).
1.5:
θεὸς φῶς ἐστιν — "God is light." The predicate noun φῶς (light) is anarthrous — it identifies the character of God without reducing him to an abstraction. In Greek predicate-noun constructions, the anarthrous noun specifies the category or quality; the subject retains its definite identity. The statement is absolute: not "God is like light" or "God gives light" but "God = light" in his essential character.

σκοτία ἐν αὐτῷ οὐκ ἔστιν οὐδεμία — the double negative is emphatic: "darkness in him is not, not even any." The MKT renders "no darkness at all," correctly capturing the intensified negation. Darkness is not a partial condition or a limited defect — it is utterly absent.
1.6:
This verse opens the first of six ἐὰν εἴπωμεν ("if we say") conditions in chapters 1–2 (1:6,8,10; 2:4,6,9). Each pairs a false verbal claim with a behavioral reality that contradicts it. The present tense περιπατῶμεν ἐν τῇ σκοτίᾳ — "we walk in darkness" — signals habitual, ongoing conduct, not isolated acts.

ψευδόμεθα — present "we lie," not "we were mistaken." The verdict is active moral falsehood. οὐ ποιοῦμεν τὴν ἀλήθειαν — "we do not do (practice) the truth." In Johannine usage, truth is not merely believed but enacted — a behavioral category as much as an epistemic one.
1.8: ἁμαρτίαν οὐκ ἔχομεν — "we have no sin." The claim is about possessing or carrying sin as a present condition, not about committing specific sinful acts (contrast v.10, "we have not sinned"). The present-tense πλανῶμεν — "we deceive" — is reflexive: the deception is self-directed. ἡ ἀλήθεια οὐκ ἔστιν ἐν ἡμῖν — "the truth is not in us." Truth (ἡ ἀλήθεια, with article) is not a proposition but a living reality with personal character in Johannine usage.
1.10: ἡμαρτήκαμεν — perfect "we have sinned" — not the present sinful condition of v.8 but the denial of specific, completed sinful acts: claiming a clean record, not merely a clean present state. ψεύστην ποιοῦμεν αὐτόν — "we make him a liar" — the charge against God himself, not merely an error. The claim that one has never sinned directly contradicts the divine testimony. ὁ λόγος αὐτοῦ οὐκ ἔστιν ἐν ἡμῖν — "his word is not in us" — an absence of indwelling personal reality, not merely intellectual disagreement.

2.1: parakletos (parakletos) — advocate, intercessor, one who is called to stand alongside — applied here to Christ as the heavenly advocate (pros ton patera, before the Father). This is the same title given to the Holy Spirit in John 14:16,26; 15:26; 16:7. The double Paraclete-theology: the Spirit as earthly advocate (present with the community) and Christ as heavenly advocate (interceding at the Father's right hand). Iesous Christos dikaios — Jesus Christ the righteous — his righteousness is the ground of his advocacy; he intercedes not on our merit but on the basis of his own righteous character and completed atonement.
2.2: hilasmos (hilasmos) — propitiation. The Greek term (from hilaskomai) carries in LXX usage the sense of satisfying divine wrath by an appointed means (cf. hilasterion = mercy seat in Exod 25:17, Rom 3:25). John uses the word twice (here and 4:10) — not the weaker expiation (removal of guilt alone) but the full propitiation (turning away wrath through sacrifice). peri holes tes kosmou — for the whole world — the scope is universal in offer while the community possesses the reality through faith.
2.18: antichristos (antichristos) — a NT coinage, not found in LXX or earlier Greek literature, appearing only in 1-2 John. Anti carries both against and in place of — the antichrist is both the opponent of Christ and the counterfeit claimant to Christ's role. The surprising claim is polloi antichristoi gegonasin — many antichrists have come — the historical manifestation of the spirit of antichrist in false teachers is the intensification and precursor of the singular eschatological figure, not a separate category. estin hote eschatou hora — it is the last hour — the eschatological urgency is grounded in this proliferation.
2.20: chrisma (chrisma) — anointing, the noun from chriein (to anoint), same root as Christos. The community has received an anointing from the Holy One — the Spirit as the christological anointing now shared with all believers (the democratization of the messianic anointing, fulfilling Joel 2:28). oidate pantes — you all know — the Spirit-anointing produces universal knowledge of divine truth within the community, not an elite gnosis reserved for some. This is the ground for distinguishing truth from error without needing outside authority.
2.27: to chrisma ho elabete — the anointing you received — aorist, a past definitive reception, now remaining (menei, present tense). The indwelling Spirit-anointing is the community's permanent possession and teacher. didaskei hymas peri panton — teaches you about all things — the Spirit's scope is comprehensive doctrinal formation. alethe estin kai ouk estin pseudos — it is true and is no lie — the Spirit's testimony is epistemically certain, contrasted with the lies of the false teachers whose spirit is deceptive.
2.3: ἐγνώκαμεν αὐτόν — perfect of γινώσκω: "we have come to know him" — a completed past event (the initial knowing) whose result (the ongoing knowledge) stands. This contrasts with the claim of v.4 (ἔγνωκα αὐτόν, also perfect but boasted without corresponding behavior). Both verses use the same form; the difference lies in the behavioral evidence John requires. ἐὰν τὰς ἐντολὰς αὐτοῦ τηρῶμεν — τηρέω (G5083) = to guard, maintain, observe — not merely to obey once but to hold in custody. Present subjunctive implies habitual keeping.
2.4: ψεύστης — "a liar." The word is blunt and judicial. In 1 John, ψεύστης is applied to those who deny the Son (2:22), those who claim to love God while hating their brother (4:20), and here to those whose verbal claim of knowing Christ is contradicted by behavior. The category is not "mistaken" but "falsifying." ἡ ἀλήθεια ἐν τούτῳ οὐκ ἔστιν — truth as a living indwelling reality, absent from this person (cf. 1:8 and 1:10).
2.5: ὃς δ᾽ ἂν τηρῇ αὐτοῦ τὸν λόγον — "but whoever keeps his word." The shift from ἐντολάς (commandments, plural, vv.3-4) to λόγον (word, singular) is significant: keeping the word is keeping the total message, not merely rule-following. ἀληθῶς ἐν τούτῳ ἡ ἀγάπη τοῦ θεοῦ τετελείωται — "the love of God has been truly perfected in this person." Τετελείωται: perfect passive of τελειόω — "has been brought to its goal." Ἀγάπη τοῦ θεοῦ — the genitive is probably subjective (God's love reaching its intended expression in us), though both readings are grammatically possible.
2.6:
ὁ λέγων ἐν αὐτῷ μένειν — "the one who says he abides in him." Μένω (G3306) in 1 John is not static dwelling but dynamic staying — choosing continually to remain in the relationship. The one who claims this abiding ὀφείλει... περιπατεῖν — "is obligated to walk." Ὀφείλω (G3784) is a term of moral debt: not "it would be nice" but "owes it."

καθὼς ἐκεῖνος περιεπάτησεν — "just as that one walked." The Greek ἐκεῖνος (that one) is a demonstrative pronoun John uses specifically for Jesus in elevated, almost confessional contexts (3:3,5,7,16; 4:17). The aorist περιεπάτησεν summarizes Jesus' entire earthly life as a completed pattern for imitation.
2.7: ἀγαπητοί (agapētoi) — "Beloved." The word is the adjective form of ἀγάπη, meaning those who are loved — not merely friends by social circumstance but those within the embrace of covenant love. ἐντολὴν παλαιάν — παλαιός (G3820) = old in the sense of long-standing, ancient. The old command (love one another) dates from "the beginning" — probably the beginning of the community's reception of the gospel, not creation. The old command is the message they heard — obedience is tied to the original proclamation.
2.8: ἐντολὴν καινήν — the same commandment is also new. Καινός (G2537) = new in quality or character, not merely recent — as distinct from νέος (new in time). The newness is demonstrated by its truth being realized: ὅ ἐστιν ἀληθὲς ἐν αὐτῷ καὶ ἐν ὑμῖν — "which is true in him and in you." The command has proved itself real in Christ's life and is now proving itself real in the readers' community. ἡ σκοτία παράγεται καὶ τὸ φῶς τὸ ἀληθινόν ἤδη φαίνει — present tenses: "the darkness is passing away, and the true light is already shining." The command's newness rests on the eschatological overlap: the light is already present.
2.9: The construction mirrors v.4: a false claim (ὁ λέγων, "the one who says") confronted by behavioral contradiction. ἕως ἄρτι — "until now/still." Even now, in the age of the already-shining light, this person remains in darkness. The present-tense ἐστίν is emphatic: regardless of any verbal claim, this is their present location.
2.10: ὁ ἀγαπῶν τὸν ἀδελφὸν αὐτοῦ ἐν τῷ φωτί μένει — "the one who loves his brother abides in the light." Love is the evidence of being in the light — not the cause of it, but the proof. σκάνδαλον ἐν αὐτῷ οὐκ ἔστιν — "there is no stumbling block in him." Σκάνδαλον (G4625): originally the trigger of a trap; here, what would cause another to fall. The love-abider is safe not only for themselves but for others.
2.11: The one who hates is located in three consecutive darkness phrases: ἐν τῇ σκοτίᾳ ἐστίν (is in the darkness), ἐν τῇ σκοτίᾳ περιπατεῖ (walks in the darkness), οὐκ οἶδεν ποῦ ὑπάγει (does not know where he is going). The triple pattern is rhetorical — each clause reinforces total disorientation. ἡ σκοτία ἐτύφλωσεν τοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς αὐτοῦ — "the darkness has blinded his eyes" (aorist — a completed blinding). The hater has been rendered unable to find a way out.
2.12: Verses 12–14 form a structured threefold address (children, fathers, young men), repeated at vv.13b-14. ἀφέωνται ὑμῖν αἱ ἁμαρτίαι — "your sins have been forgiven" (perfect passive — a past event with present standing). διὰ τὸ ὄνομα αὐτοῦ — "on account of his name." The name carries the full identity and authority of the person; forgiveness is mediated through the specific person of Jesus, not an abstract principle.
2.13: τὸν ἀπ᾽ ἀρχῆς — "him who is from the beginning." The fathers have known the pre-existent one — echoing 1:1. The depth of their knowing is proportioned to the duration of the relationship with the one who is eternally prior. τὸν πονηρόν (G4190, "the evil one") — with article, a personal referent: Satan, not abstract evil. The young men have "overcome" (νενικήκατε, perfect) the personal adversary — a completed victory that stands.
2.14: The tense shifts from γράφω (present "I write," vv.12-13a) to ἔγραψα (aorist "I wrote," vv.13b-14). This is the epistolary aorist — the author writes from the future perspective of the reader, who will receive it as a past communication. It is not a reference to a different earlier letter. ὁ λόγος τοῦ θεοῦ ἐν ὑμῖν μένει — "the word of God abides in you." The word is not merely known but dwelling (μένω, present) — the same indwelling language applied to God himself in later chapters.
2.15: μὴ ἀγαπᾶτε τὸν κόσμον — present imperative with negation: "do not make a habit of loving the world." Κόσμος (G2889) — in Johannine usage, the world as organized in opposition to God, not the creation per se (cf. 4:9 where God loves the world and 4:14 where the Son is Savior of the world). ἡ ἀγάπη τοῦ πατρός — "the love of the Father" — whether objective (our love for the Father) or subjective (the Father's love flowing in us) — is incompatible with world-love. The verb ἀγαπάω is the same for both loves; the objects define the incompatibility.
2.16: Three elements of the κόσμος: (1) ἡ ἐπιθυμία τῆς σαρκός — desire generated by the bodily/fallen nature; (2) ἡ ἐπιθυμία τῶν ὀφθαλμῶν — desire triggered by what is seen and coveted; (3) ἡ ἀλαζονεία τοῦ βίου — the arrogant display (ἀλαζονεία, G212) of material possessions and status (βίος = material means of living, distinct from ζωή = spiritual life). All three οὐκ ἔστιν ἐκ τοῦ πατρός — "is not from the Father." The preposition ἐκ traces origin: these are not God-originated desires.
2.17: ὁ κόσμος παράγεται — "the world is passing away" (present, ongoing). The same verb as 2:8 — the darkness also is passing. Both the cosmos and its craving are in the process of dissolution. ὁ δὲ ποιῶν τὸ θέλημα τοῦ θεοῦ μένει εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα — "the one who does the will of God abides into the age." Εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα = "forever" (literally "into the age"). The contrast is stark: the world and its craving are transient; the God-doer has the permanence of divine life.
2.19: ἐξ ἡμῶν ἐξῆλθαν — "they went out from us." The departure itself is the evidence. ἀλλ᾽ οὐκ ἦσαν ἐξ ἡμῶν — "but they were not of us." The preposition ἐκ (of, from) identifies origin and therefore belonging: to be of the community is to belong to it constitutively, not just numerically. εἰ γὰρ ἐξ ἡμῶν ἦσαν, μεμενήκεισαν ἂν μεθ᾽ ἡμῶν — a second-class conditional (contrary to fact): they were not of us, therefore they did not remain. The departure was a disclosure, not a surprise.
2.21: The verse gives the ground for writing: not ignorance but existing knowledge. πᾶν ψεῦδος ἐκ τῆς ἀληθείας οὐκ ἔστιν — "every lie is not from the truth." The double negative pattern is emphatic: no lie whatsoever has its origin in the truth. Lie and truth have different sources (ἐκ), not different qualities of the same stream.
2.22: ψεύστης revisited. The supreme liar is the one who denies the central christological claim: ὁ ἀρνούμενος ὅτι Ἰησοῦς οὐκ ἔστιν ὁ Χριστός — "the one who denies that Jesus is the Christ." The denial has structural consequences: οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ ἀντίχριστος ὁ ἀρνούμενος τὸν πατέρα καὶ τὸν υἱόν — to deny the Son is simultaneously to deny the Father. The Father–Son relation is not separable: one cannot hold the Father while rejecting the Son.
2.23: πᾶς ὁ ἀρνούμενος τὸν υἱὸν οὐδὲ τὸν πατέρα ἔχει — "everyone who denies the Son does not even have the Father." Ἔχω here = possessing in a relational sense. The logic is tight: the Father is accessible only through the Son. ὁ ὁμολογῶν τὸν υἱὸν καὶ τὸν πατέρα ἔχει — "the one who confesses the Son has the Father also." Ὁμολογέω here: not just affirming truth but openly declaring allegiance — a public, verbal act.
2.24:
ὑμεῖς ὃ ἠκούσατε ἀπ᾽ ἀρχῆς — the appeal to "what you heard from the beginning" returns to the letter's opening framework (1:1). The readers' anchor is apostolic testimony received at conversion, not new revelations from departing teachers. ἐν ὑμῖν μενέτω — imperative: "let it remain in you." The readers are commanded to ensure the message stays in residence.

ἐν τῷ υἱῷ καὶ ἐν τῷ πατρί μενεῖτε — "you will remain in the Son and in the Father." The conditional structure: if the message abides in them, they will abide in the divine persons. Doctrinal faithfulness and relational indwelling are inseparable in Johannine theology.
2.25: ἡ ἐπαγγελία ἥν αὐτὸς ἐπηγγείλατο ἡμῖν — "the promise that he himself promised us." The cognate accusative construction (ἐπαγγελία ... ἐπηγγείλατο) emphasizes the directness: this is exactly what he committed to. τὴν ζωὴν τὴν αἰώνιον — "the eternal life" (definite article, as in 1:2). The promise returns to the letter's opening — the eternal life that was with the Father and was manifested is now the promised destination for those who remain.
2.26: A transitional verse explaining the foregoing polemic. τῶν πλανώντων ὑμᾶς — "those who lead you astray" (πλανάω, G4105): those who cause to wander. The language of deception and wandering frames the false teachers' activity throughout 1 John. John writes not to correct ignorance but to arm against active, ongoing deceivers.
2.28: νῦν, τεκνία, μένετε ἐν αὐτῷ — "now, dear children, abide in him." The imperative μένετε pulls together the abiding theme of the chapter. ἵνα ἐὰν φανερωθῇ σχῶμεν παρρησίαν — "so that when he is revealed we may have boldness." Παρρησία (G3954): literally "all-speech" — the freedom of unrestricted speech, here the eschatological confidence of the community at the parousia, contrasted with αἰσχυνθῶμεν ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ — "shrink in shame from him." The verb αἰσχύνω connotes the social shame of public exposure.
2.29: ἐὰν εἰδῆτε ὅτι δίκαιός ἐστιν — "if you know that he is righteous." The condition is not in doubt; the ἐάν introduces an inference. γινώσκετε ὅτι πᾶς ὁ ποιῶν τὴν δικαιοσύνην ἐξ αὐτοῦ γεγέννηται — "know that everyone who practices righteousness has been born of him." Γεγέννηται: perfect passive of γεννάω — "has been born" — a past completed act with present standing. The new birth is the ground of righteous practice; practice is the evidence of birth, not its cause. This verse bridges chapter 2 to chapter 3's extended development of the born-of-God theme.

3.1: idete potapen agapen — see what kind of love — potapos (what kind, what country of origin) is a word of astonishment at the foreign, exotic quality of this love; it is not ordinary human love scaled up but something categorically from another source. techna theou klethenomen — called children of God — the verb kaleo (to call) carries the sense of naming into reality; God's calling makes the adoption real. kai esmen — and we are — the parenthetical reassurance that the name is not mere title but ontological reality.
3.2: homoioi auto esometha — we will be like him — the most concentrated theosis-language in the NT epistles. Homoioi does not mean numerically identical but sharing in the likeness of his glory. hoti opsometha auton kathos estin — because we will see him as he is — the causal structure is remarkable: vision produces transformation. The beatific vision is not the reward for prior perfection but the means by which the transformation occurs. This inverts platonic hierarchy (ethics first, then vision) by making the eschatological vision itself the transformative event.
3.4: hamartia estin he anomia — sin is lawlessness (literally: sin is the lawlessness) — the article makes this a definitional equation: whatever sin is, it is by nature anomia (not-law, anti-law, lawlessness). This is not merely breaking a rule but a posture of anti-covenantal defiance. The definition excludes any category of sin as merely accidental or excusable social error; all sin stands in the same relation to God's law as deliberate covenant-breaking.
3.8: lyse (luo) — destroy, loose, undo — the verb used for dissolving bonds, releasing captives, and dismantling structures. The Son of God appeared (ephanerothe) for this purpose: the unraveling of the devil's entire project. ta erga tou diabolou — the works of the devil — not merely individual sins but the system-of-works that the devil has constructed: deception, accusation, death, bondage. The same verb appears in Eph 2:14 (Christ loosed the dividing wall) and in eschatological contexts of dissolution.
3.14: metabebakamen ek tou thanatou eis ten zoen — we have passed from death to life (perfect tense, completed transition still in effect) — the perfect tense indicates a decisive past event with permanent present result. The criterion given is hoti agapomen tous adelphous — because we love the brothers — love is not the cause of the passage but the evidence and test of whether the passage has occurred. The one who does not love (ho me agapon) remains (menei) in death; love and life are inseparable Johannine realities.
3.3: ἁγνίζει ἑαυτόν — "purifies himself." Ἁγνίζω (G48) — to make pure; related to ἅγιος (holy) but with emphasis on the moral purity that distinguishes what is consecrated. The active voice and reflexive ἑαυτόν: the believer participates in their own purification — the hope motivates a present ethical response. καθὼς ἐκεῖνος ἁγνός ἐστιν — "just as that one is pure." Ἐκεῖνος — John's elevated demonstrative for Christ (see 2:6). Present-tense ἐστίν: Christ's purity is his ongoing state, the standard being aimed at.
3.5: ἐφανερώθη — "was manifested" (aorist passive). The appearance of Christ serves a specific purpose: ἵνα τὰς ἁμαρτίας ἄρῃ — "to take away sins." Αἴρω (G142, aorist subjunctive here) = to lift, carry away — not merely to cover or forgive but to remove. The same verb appears in John 1:29 ("Behold, the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world"). ἁμαρτία ἐν αὐτῷ οὐκ ἔστιν — "in him there is no sin" (anarthrous, emphatic). His sinlessness is the ground for his sin-removing work.
3.6:
πᾶς ὁ ἐν αὐτῷ μένων οὐχ ἁμαρτάνει — "everyone who abides in him does not sin." Both μένων (abiding) and ἁμαρτάνει (sins) are present participle and present indicative — ongoing states. The claim is about habitual pattern, not sinless perfection (cf. 1:8-10). The one whose characteristic mode of existence is abiding does not have sin as their characteristic mode of existence.

πᾶς ὁ ἁμαρτάνων οὐχ ἑώρακεν αὐτὸν οὐδὲ ἔγνωκεν αὐτόν — "everyone who sins has not seen him nor known him." Perfect tenses: the absence of genuine past experience of Christ is evidenced in the present pattern of sin.
3.7: μηδεὶς πλανάτω ὑμᾶς — "let no one lead you astray." Present imperative of πλανάω — the same verb as 2:26 (πλανώντων). The danger is ongoing. ὁ ποιῶν τὴν δικαιοσύνην δίκαιός ἐστιν — "the one who does righteousness is righteous." Greek identifies the person by their practice: there is no righteousness status that exists apart from righteous practice. Δικαιοσύνη (G1343, dispute_level 4) — here in its behavioral sense (right-living), though the forensic root (righteous standing before God) underlies it.
3.9:
πᾶς ὁ γεγεννημένος ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ ἁμαρτίαν οὐ ποιεῖ — "everyone who has been born of God does not practice sin." Γεγεννημένος: perfect passive participle — "the one who has been born" — the birth is a completed, ongoing state. The sinning (ποιεῖ, present) that is denied is habitual practice.

ὅτι σπέρμα αὐτοῦ ἐν αὐτῷ μένει — "because his seed remains in him." Σπέρμα (G4690): the "seed" of God. Three main interpretations exist: (1) the divine nature itself; (2) the Holy Spirit as agent of new birth; (3) the word of God as instrument of regeneration (cf. James 1:18; 1 Pet 1:23). All three find support in Johannine theology; the term may be deliberately polyvalent. The point: the divine generative principle abides (μένει, present) in the born-of-God person, making sin-as-lifestyle impossible.
3.10: ἐν τούτῳ φανερά ἐστιν τὰ τέκνα τοῦ θεοῦ καὶ τὰ τέκνα τοῦ διαβόλου — "in this the children of God and the children of the devil are manifest." Φανερός (evident, visible) uses the phan- root running through the letter. The two families become visible by their characteristic practices. The criteria are twofold: δικαιοσύνη (righteousness) and ἀγάπη τοῦ ἀδελφοῦ (love of the brother). Neither alone suffices; together they constitute the distinguishing mark.
3.11: ἀγγελία (angelia, G31) — "message," appearing only here and at 1:5 in the NT. The letter frames its two great declarations as messages received and transmitted: "God is light" (1:5) and "we should love one another" (3:11). Both are sourced in apostolic proclamation from the beginning — not new revelations but original announcements. ἀπ᾽ ἀρχῆς — "from the beginning" — the same phrase as 1:1, 2:7,13,14,24.
3.12: Κάιν — Cain appears only here in the NT epistles as a type (Heb 11:4 mentions Abel). ἐκ τοῦ πονηροῦ ἦν — "was of the evil one" (ἐκ, origin/allegiance). ἔσφαξεν (G4969) — "slew/slaughtered." The verb is used in Revelation for the slaughter of the Lamb — a pointed contrast with the lamb whose blood cleanses. χάριν τίνος ἔσφαξεν αὐτόν — "for what reason did he slaughter him?" John answers: ὅτι τὰ ἔργα αὐτοῦ πονηρὰ ἦν τὰ δὲ τοῦ ἀδελφοῦ αὐτοῦ δίκαια — "because his works were evil and his brother's were righteous." Righteousness provokes murderous hatred in those whose origin is the evil one.
3.13: μὴ θαυμάζετε — "do not be surprised/marvel" (present imperative with negation). World hatred of believers is not anomalous but structurally predictable: Cain established the pattern. εἰ μισεῖ ὑμᾶς ὁ κόσμος — "if the world hates you." The εἰ + indicative is a first-class condition — the reality of the world's hatred is assumed, not hypothetical.
3.15: πᾶς ὁ μισῶν τὸν ἀδελφὸν αὐτοῦ ἀνθρωποκτόνος ἐστίν — "everyone who hates his brother is a murderer." The move from Cain's external act to the internal disposition reflects Jesus' logic in Matt 5:21-22, now formalized as a categorical identification. Ἀνθρωποκτόνος (G443) = man-killer; the same word used in John 8:44 for the devil. οὐδεὶς ἀνθρωποκτόνος ἔχει ζωὴν αἰώνιον ἐν αὐτῷ μένουσαν — "no murderer has eternal life remaining in him." Μένουσαν — present participle: the life does not have residence there.
3.16:
ἐν τούτῳ ἐγνώκαμεν τὴν ἀγάπην — "by this we have come to know love." Ἐγνώκαμεν: perfect of γινώσκω — we have come to know and continue to know. The object is not a concept but a reality disclosed by a specific historical act. ὅτι ἐκεῖνος ὑπὲρ ἡμῶν τὴν ψυχὴν αὐτοῦ ἔθηκεν — "because that one laid down his life for us." Ἐκεῖνος — John's elevated demonstrative for Christ. Τίθημι τὴν ψυχήν = "to lay down the soul/life" — the identical phrase as John 10:11,15,17,18. The act was voluntary and unrepeated (aorist ἔθηκεν).

καὶ ἡμεῖς ὀφείλομεν ὑπὲρ τῶν ἀδελφῶν τὰς ψυχὰς θεῖναι — "and we are obligated to lay down our lives for the brothers and sisters." Ὀφείλομεν — moral obligation (G3784), same as 2:6. The cross is not only the ground of forgiveness but the pattern for community life.
3.17: ὃς δ᾽ ἂν ἔχῃ τὸν βίον τοῦ κόσμου — "whoever has the world's possessions." Βίος (G979) — material means of living, distinct from ζωή (spiritual life). θεωρῇ τὸν ἀδελφὸν αὐτοῦ χρείαν ἔχοντα — "and sees his brother having need." Θεωρέω = to behold carefully, observe attentively — a present participle of watching, not mere noticing. κλείσῃ τὰ σπλάγχνα αὐτοῦ ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ — "shuts his compassion from him." Σπλάγχνα (G4698): entrails — the Greek seat of deep emotion and compassion (as English uses "gut" or "heart"). To shut the splancha is to clamp down on the movement of compassion. πῶς ἡ ἀγάπη τοῦ θεοῦ μένει ἐν αὐτῷ — "how does God's love remain in him?" The question is rhetorical: it cannot.
3.18: μὴ ἀγαπῶμεν λόγῳ μηδὲ τῇ γλώσσῃ — "let us not love in word nor in tongue." Two instruments of verbal expression — λόγος (the utterance, the content) and γλῶσσα (the tongue, the organ). ἀλλὰ ἐν ἔργῳ καὶ ἀληθείᾳ — "but in deed and in truth." The contrast is not between speech and silence but between empty declaration and enacted reality. In Johannine usage, truth is not merely propositional but a reality that must be lived (ποιεῖν τὴν ἀλήθειαν, "doing the truth," 1:6).
3.19: ἐν τούτῳ γνωσόμεθα ὅτι ἐκ τῆς ἀληθείας ἐσμέν — "by this we will know that we are of the truth." Γνωσόμεθα (future of γινώσκω) — assurance comes from observing love-in-deed, not from introspection alone. Ἐκ τῆς ἀληθείας — origin again: to be "of the truth" means to have truth as one's source. καὶ ἔμπροσθεν αὐτοῦ πείσομεν τὰς καρδίας ἡμῶν — "and before him we shall persuade/set at rest our hearts." Πείθω (G3982) — to persuade, convince. We argue our own hearts toward confidence in God's presence, using the evidence of love enacted.
3.20: ὅτι ἐὰν καταγινώσκῃ ἡμῶν ἡ καρδία — "if our heart condemns us." Καταγινώσκω (G2607) — to know against, to judge against; the heart acting as prosecutor against oneself. ὅτι μείζων ἐστὶν ὁ θεὸς τῆς καρδίας ἡμῶν καὶ γινώσκει πάντα — "because God is greater than our heart and knows all things." Two arguments: God is greater than the condemning heart in authority, and his knowledge is total. His omniscience is not a further threat but the ground for appeal: comprehensive divine knowledge will not miss the genuine love enacted, even when conscience cannot assess it rightly.
3.21: ἐὰν ἡ καρδία μὴ καταγινώσκῃ — "if our heart does not condemn us." The positive corollary of v.20. παρρησίαν ἔχομεν πρὸς τὸν θεόν — "we have boldness toward God." Παρρησία (G3954) — literally "all-speech" — the freedom of unrestricted speech before God. In 1 John it appears at 2:28 (at the parousia), 3:21 (present prayer), and 5:14 (prayer according to his will). The accusative-of-direction πρὸς τὸν θεόν frames prayer as confident approach, not fearful petition.
3.22: ὃ ἐὰν αἰτῶμεν λαμβάνομεν ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ — "whatever we ask we receive from him." The claim is not unconditional: the ground is given in the ὅτι clause — τὰς ἐντολὰς αὐτοῦ τηροῦμεν καὶ τὰ ἀρεστὰ ἐνώπιον αὐτοῦ ποιοῦμεν — "because we keep his commandments and do what is pleasing in his sight." Ἀρεστά (G701, pleasing things) and commandment-keeping together characterize the relational context in which effective prayer operates. Answered prayer is a feature of abiding relationship, not a technique.
3.23: αὕτη ἐστὶν ἡ ἐντολὴ αὐτοῦ — "this is his commandment" (singular). The plural commandments of vv.22-24 are summed up as one: ἵνα πιστεύσωμεν τῷ ὀνόματι τοῦ υἱοῦ αὐτοῦ Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ καὶ ἀγαπῶμεν ἀλλήλους — "that we believe in the name of his Son Jesus Christ and love one another." Two verbs, one command: faith and love as inseparable. Πιστεύειν τῷ ὀνόματι — "to believe in the name" uses the dative of trust — the name carries the person; to trust the name is to trust the one who bears it.
3.24: ὁ τηρῶν τὰς ἐντολὰς αὐτοῦ ἐν αὐτῷ μένει καὶ αὐτὸς ἐν αὐτῷ — "the one who keeps his commandments abides in him and he in him." The mutual indwelling is stated symmetrically: commandment-keeping → human-in-God abiding → God-in-human abiding. The structure is reciprocal but not equivalent: the human keeps commands; God grants the indwelling. ἐκ τοῦ πνεύματος οὗ ἡμῖν ἔδωκεν — "by the Spirit which he has given us." The Spirit (G4151, capitalized — the Holy Spirit, as in the translation decisions) is the tangible evidence of divine indwelling. The gift is a completed action (ἔδωκεν, aorist) with ongoing implications.

4.2: Iesoun Christon en sarki elelythota — Jesus Christ having come in the flesh — the perfect participle elelythota (from erchomai) indicates not merely a past event but a permanent state: having come and continuing to be in the state of having-come-in-flesh. The incarnation is not a temporary episode but a permanent mode of existence (the risen Christ is still the enfleshed Christ). This is the anti-Docetic criterion: spirits denying this permanent incarnate state are not from God.
4.8: ho theos agape estin — God is love — one of only two direct ontological predications of this type in the NT (the other is "God is spirit," John 4:24). The syntax is not reversible: love is not God, but God is love — love is a property of God's essential being, not God's definition from love's perspective. This grounds the entire argument: love's origin (ek tou theou = from God), love's standard (the Son's self-giving), and love's purpose (mutual abiding) all flow from this ontological claim.
4.16: ho menon en te agape en to theo menei kai ho theos en auto menei — the one abiding in love abides in God and God abides in him — the mutual-indwelling formula reaches its full expression. Meno (abide, remain, dwell) is the signature Johannine verb: vine-and-branches (John 15), Spirit-dwelling (John 14:17), Father-Son indwelling (John 14:10), and now the community's love-abiding. The grammar is concentric: love → God → mutual indwelling; leaving love = leaving God.
4.18: teleia agape exo ballei ton phobon — perfect love throws out fear — ekballo is the standard verb for exorcism in the Synoptic Gospels. Perfect love casts out fear the way Jesus cast out demons: decisively, authoritatively, completely. kolasis — punishment, torment (not the general word for punishment but the word for painful suffering) — fear has to do with anticipated kolasis, which produces the tormented life of the not-yet-perfected. ho de phoboumenos ou teteleiōtai en te agape — the one fearing has not been perfected in love — fear is diagnostic of incomplete love formation.
4.19: hemeis agapomen hoti autos protos egapesen hemas — we love because he first loved us — the hoti is causal: the prevenience of divine love is the sole sufficient cause of human love-capacity. There is no human love for God that did not first receive love from God; the entire ethical project of 1 John rests on the initiative of God's love in the sending of the Son. The comparative protos (first) is temporal and logical: God's love precedes, initiates, and enables ours.
4.1: μὴ παντὶ πνεύματι πιστεύετε — "do not believe every spirit." Πνεῦμα (G4151, lowercase here — created or supernatural spirits, including those motivating false prophets). The imperative πιστεύετε with μή = "stop trusting" or "do not habitually trust." δοκιμάζετε τὰ πνεύματα — "test the spirits." Δοκιμάζω (G1381) = to test by experience, to examine in order to approve or reject — the verb used for testing metals to determine their genuineness. ὅτι πολλοὶ ψευδοπροφῆται ἐξεληλύθασιν εἰς τὸν κόσμον — perfect: "have gone out into the world" and remain there.
4.3: πᾶν πνεῦμα ὃ μὴ ὁμολογεῖ τὸν Ἰησοῦν ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ οὐκ ἔστιν — "every spirit that does not confess Jesus is not from God." The object shifts: not "Jesus Christ in flesh" but simply "Jesus" — the particular historical person. Μή + present indicative: any spirit whose ongoing, habitual stance is to not acknowledge Jesus. τοῦτό ἐστιν τὸ τοῦ ἀντιχρίστου — "this is (the spirit) of the antichrist." The antichrist's spirit is recognizable by this specific christological deficiency. ἤδη ἐν τῷ κόσμῳ ἐστίν — "it is already in the world." The eschatological adversary is not only future but presently active.
4.4: ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ ἐστε — "you are of God." The community's origin (ἐκ) is God, contrasted with the false spirits. νενικήκατε αὐτούς — "you have overcome them" (perfect: completed victory with ongoing standing). ὅτι μείζων ἐστὶν ὁ ἐν ὑμῖν ἢ ὁ ἐν τῷ κόσμῳ — "because greater is the one in you than the one in the world." A comparison between two personal powers: the Spirit of God dwelling in believers (cf. 3:24) and the devil / spirit of antichrist operating in the world. The victory rests not in human resistance but in the superior power of the indwelling Spirit.
4.5: αὐτοὶ ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου εἰσίν — "they are of the world." The false teachers' origin and allegiance (ἐκ) is the world. διὰ τοῦτο ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου λαλοῦσιν καὶ ὁ κόσμος αὐτῶν ἀκούει — "therefore from the world they speak, and the world listens to them." Three consecutive κόσμος uses: origin, content, audience all share the same sphere. The world's receptiveness to the false teachers is itself a diagnostic — if the κόσμος as organized opposition to God welcomes a teaching, that is evidence against its divine origin.
4.6: ἡμεῖς ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ ἐσμεν — emphatic "we are of God" by pronoun position, contrasted with v.5's "they are of the world." ὁ γινώσκων τὸν θεὸν ἀκούει ἡμῶν — "the one who knows God listens to us." Receptiveness to the apostolic witness correlates with orientation toward God. ἐκ τούτου γινώσκομεν τὸ πνεῦμα τῆς ἀληθείας καὶ τὸ πνεῦμα τῆς πλάνης — "by this we know the spirit of truth and the spirit of error." Πλάνη (G4106) — wandering, error. Two spirits, identified by their relationship to apostolic truth and their audience.
4.7: Ἀγαπητοί, ἀγαπῶμεν ἀλλήλους — "Beloved, let us love one another." The hortatory subjunctive ἀγαπῶμεν opens the letter's sustained theological treatment of love. ὅτι ἡ ἀγάπη ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ ἐστιν — "because love is from God." The preposition ἐκ: love originates in God; it is not a human achievement transplanted to God. πᾶς ὁ ἀγαπῶν ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ γεγέννηται καὶ γινώσκει τὸν θεόν — "everyone who loves has been born of God and knows God." Two results: born of God (perfect, settled state) and knows God (present, ongoing relationship).
4.9: ἐν τούτῳ ἐφανερώθη ἡ ἀγάπη τοῦ θεοῦ ἐν ἡμῖν — "in this the love of God was manifested among us." Φανερόω (aorist) — the same manifestation-verb throughout 1 John (1:2; 3:5,8). God's love became visible in a historical event. ὅτι τὸν υἱὸν αὐτοῦ τὸν μονογενῆ ἀπέσταλκεν ὁ θεὸς εἰς τὸν κόσμον — "because God has sent his only-begotten Son into the world." Ἀπέσταλκεν: perfect of ἀποστέλλω — a past sending with present effect. Μονογενής (G3439) — "only-begotten" or "unique" — carries both uniqueness and the relational intimacy of the only son. The sending into the world (εἰς τὸν κόσμον) is costly in the Johannine frame where κόσμος is hostile territory.
4.10: ἐν τούτῳ ἐστὶν ἡ ἀγάπη — "in this is love" — love's essence is being defined, not merely illustrated. οὐχ ὅτι ἡμεῖς ἠγαπήσαμεν τὸν θεόν — "not that we loved God." Aorist — the focus is on initiative, not ongoing pattern: who acted first. ἀλλ᾽ ὅτι αὐτὸς ἠγάπησεν ἡμᾶς καὶ ἀπέστειλεν τὸν υἱὸν αὐτοῦ ἱλασμὸν περὶ τῶν ἁμαρτιῶν ἡμῶν — "but that he loved us and sent his Son as propitiation for our sins." Ἱλασμόν (G2434, second and last occurrence in 1 John; cf. 2:2) — the Son sent to be a hilasmos, a propitiation. Both aorists (ἠγάπησεν, ἀπέστειλεν) frame the prior divine initiative as a single historical complex of love-and-sending.
4.11: Ἀγαπητοί, εἰ οὕτως ὁ θεὸς ἠγάπησεν ἡμᾶς — "Beloved, if God so loved us." The εἰ + aorist indicative is a first-class condition: the reality is assumed as the basis for inference. καὶ ἡμεῖς ὀφείλομεν ἀλλήλους ἀγαπᾶν — "we also are obligated to love one another." Ὀφείλω (G3784) — moral obligation — same as 2:6 and 3:16. The obligation derives from received love of God; it is not a separate ethical principle but the logical output of being loved. Chain: God → loved us → we → obligated to love one another.
4.12: θεὸν οὐδεὶς πώποτε τεθέαται — "God no one has ever beheld." Τεθέαται — perfect of θεάομαι (G2300), to behold carefully or gaze upon. The same claim as John 1:18 (θεὸν οὐδεὶς ἑώρακεν πώποτε). Πώποτε — "at any time, ever" — emphatic absolute exclusion. Since God remains unseen by direct vision, the love of believers for one another becomes the visible form of his indwelling. ἐὰν ἀγαπῶμεν ἀλλήλους, ὁ θεὸς ἐν ἡμῖν μένει — the conditional is real: mutual love is both the sign and the condition of divine indwelling. ἡ ἀγάπη αὐτοῦ τετελειωμένη ἐν ἡμῖν ἐστίν — "his love has been perfected in us" (perfect passive: a completed state). God's love has reached its goal in the community of mutual love.
4.13: ἐν τούτῳ γινώσκομεν ὅτι ἐν αὐτῷ μένομεν καὶ αὐτὸς ἐν ἡμῖν — "by this we know that we abide in him and he in us." ὅτι ἐκ τοῦ πνεύματος αὐτοῦ δέδωκεν ἡμῖν — "because of his Spirit he has given us." Ἐκ τοῦ πνεύματος — "of/from his Spirit." The construction is partitive: God has given us a portion from his Spirit — not the Spirit divided, but the Spirit given in the mode of gift. Perfect δέδωκεν: the giving is complete and its result stands.
4.14: ἡμεῖς τεθεάμεθα καὶ μαρτυροῦμεν — "we have seen and we testify." Τεθεάμεθα — perfect of θεάομαι, same verb as v.12 for the unseen God: the apostolic witnesses have seen what ordinary observers cannot. Μαρτυροῦμεν — present: the testimony is ongoing. ὅτι ὁ πατὴρ ἀπέσταλκεν τὸν υἱὸν σωτῆρα τοῦ κόσμου — "that the Father has sent the Son as Savior of the world." Perfect ἀπέσταλκεν as in v.9: the sending stands. Σωτῆρα τοῦ κόσμου — the same κόσμος that is hostile to God is also the object of the Son's saving mission.
4.15: ὃς ἂν ὁμολογήσῃ ὅτι Ἰησοῦς ἐστιν ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ θεοῦ — "whoever confesses that Jesus is the Son of God." The confession is specific and public: ὁμολογέω requires verbal, articulated acknowledgment. The content is the identity of the particular historical person Jesus as God's Son. ὁ θεὸς ἐν αὐτῷ μένει καὶ αὐτὸς ἐν τῷ θεῷ — "God abides in him and he in God." The mutual indwelling formula attached to the confession criterion: Christological confession and divine indwelling are structurally linked.
4.17:
ἐν τούτῳ τετελείωται ἡ ἀγάπη μεθ᾽ ἡμῶν — "in this love has been perfected with us." Τετελείωται — perfect passive, as in 2:5 and 4:12. Love reaching its telos (goal, completion) with us — not merely in God. ἵνα παρρησίαν ἔχωμεν ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῆς κρίσεως — "so that we may have boldness in the day of judgment." Παρρησία (G3954) — the purpose of perfected love is eschatological boldness.

ὅτι καθὼς ἐκεῖνός ἐστιν καὶ ἡμεῖς ἐσμεν ἐν τῷ κόσμῳ τούτῳ — "because as that one is, so also we are in this world." Ἐκεῖνος — Christ. The present tense in both clauses: as Christ is now (in his glorified state), so we are now in this world — not will be, but are. Our identity in the world is already determined by our identification with him.
4.20: ἐάν τις εἴπῃ ὅτι Ἀγαπῶ τὸν θεόν — "if anyone says, 'I love God.'" This is the sixth if anyone says pattern in the letter (cf. 1:6,8,10; 2:4,6,9). καὶ τὸν ἀδελφὸν αὐτοῦ μισῇ — "and hates his brother." ψεύστης ἐστίν — "is a liar." The argument from the lesser to the greater: ὁ γὰρ μὴ ἀγαπῶν τὸν ἀδελφὸν αὐτοῦ ὃν ἑώρακεν, τὸν θεὸν ὃν οὐχ ἑώρακεν οὐ δύναται ἀγαπᾶν — "the one who does not love his brother whom he has seen cannot love God whom he has not seen." Visibility is the pivot: the seen brother is the available, accessible object of love; if the capacity for love fails there, it cannot exist for the unseen God.
4.21: καὶ ταύτην τὴν ἐντολὴν ἔχομεν ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ — "and this commandment we have from him." Ἔχομεν — present: the commandment is in our possession now. ἵνα ὁ ἀγαπῶν τὸν θεὸν ἀγαπᾷ καὶ τὸν ἀδελφὸν αὐτοῦ — "that the one who loves God must also love his brother." The καί (also) ties the two loves together: they are not independent commandments but one linked command. The love of God that does not issue in love of the visible sibling is not the love of the God who is love.

5.1:
πᾶς ὁ πιστεύων — "everyone believing" (present participle) makes faith an ongoing orientation. The perfect γεγέννηται ("has been born and remains in the born-state") marks the divine birth as a completed act with permanent result.

τὸν Χριστόν — the article makes this "the Christ," the full messianic title — not a generic honorific but the identification: Jesus is the Davidic anointed one Israel awaited.

ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ γεγέννηται … τὸν γεγεννημένον ἐξ αὐτοῦ — family logic: love for the Father extends necessarily to the children born of him. John grounds horizontal love for fellow believers in shared vertical origin in God.
5.4:
πᾶν τὸ γεγεννημένον ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ — neuter singular ("everything born of God") — the overcoming property belongs to the entire class. νικᾷ τὸν κόσμον — G3528 nikáō present tense: "overcomes" as an ongoing habitual reality.

ἡ νίκη ἡ νικήσασα τὸν κόσμον — the noun níkē (victory) followed immediately by the aorist participle of the same root (nikḗsasa): emphatic — the conquest-victory that already-conquered. The aorist points to a definitive past victory (Christ's death and resurrection) that the community participates in through faith.

ἡ πίστις ἡμῶν — the double article isolates "our faith" (G4102 pístis, dispute_level 3) as the specific instrument. Faith does not win the victory but connects the believer to the Victor's already-won conquest.
5.6:
ὁ ἐλθὼν δι᾽ ὕδατος καὶ αἵματος — Jesus came "through water and blood" (G5204, G129). The water is most naturally his baptism (the inauguration of ministry); the blood is his death. οὐκ ἐν τῷ ὕδατι μόνον ἀλλ᾽ ἐν τῷ ὕδατι καὶ ἐν τῷ αἵματι — not by water only but by water and blood. The emphasis counters an early Christological error (possibly Cerinthian) in which the divine Christ entered Jesus at baptism but departed before the crucifixion. John insists the same person encompassed both endpoints — the one who was baptized was crucified.

τὸ πνεῦμά ἐστιν τὸ μαρτυροῦν — the Spirit is continuously testifying (present participle). The reason: ὅτι τὸ πνεῦμά ἐστιν ἡ ἀλήθεια — G225 alḗtheia. The Spirit does not merely speak truth; the Spirit is truth, making the Spirit's testimony self-validating in a way human witness cannot be.
5.11:
αὕτη ἐστὶν ἡ μαρτυρία — "this is the testimony" — G3141 martyría is legal-forensic testimony; the cumulative witness of Spirit, water, and blood constitutes a legally sufficient case for the community's certainty.

ζωὴν αἰώνιον ἔδωκεν ἡμῖν ὁ θεός — G2222 zōḗ (dispute_level 2) with G166 aiṓnios (dispute_level 3): life belonging to the coming age, qualitatively resurrection life, not merely duration. αὕτη ἡ ζωὴ ἐν τῷ υἱῷ αὐτοῦ ἐστίν — the life is located in the Son, not distributed apart from him. Union with the Son is the only access to this life — the stark binary of v.12 follows necessarily.
5.14:
παρρησία (G3954) — in Athenian political life, the citizen's right to speak freely in the assembly; transferred here to approaching God in prayer. It means filial boldness — not presumption but the confidence of those who have access through the Son.

ἐάν τι αἰτώμεθα κατὰ τὸ θέλημα αὐτοῦ — present subjunctive aitōmetha = "whenever we ask" — a repeatable condition. G2307 thélēma (will) is the sovereign purposive will. κατὰ τὸ θέλημα αὐτοῦ is not a limiting escape clause but the governing channel through which effective prayer flows.
5.16:
ἁμαρτάνοντα ἁμαρτίαν μὴ πρὸς θάνατον — the verb G264 hamartánō and noun G266 hamartía are both used: "sinning a sin not toward death." G2065 erōtáō (pray/ask with intimacy) rather than G154 aitéō; the intercession promise applies to this category of sin.

ἔστιν ἁμαρτία πρὸς θάνατον — there is a sin toward death. John does not identify it; the phrase has generated sustained debate (apostasy? blasphemy against the Spirit? persistent rejection of the Son? some mortal sin category?). John deliberately withholds specification. The withdrawal of the intercession mandate — οὐ περὶ ἐκείνης λέγω ἵνα ἐρωτήσῃ — is unusual; the text leaves it unexplained and commentary should not resolve what John chose not to resolve.
5.20:
ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ θεοῦ ἥκει — G2240 hḗkō is a perfect-like present: "has come and is here" — arrival with present consequence. δέδωκεν ἡμῖν διάνοιαν — G1271 diánoia: reflective, discerning understanding. The perfect dédōken marks a completed gift now possessed.

τὸν ἀληθινόν — G228 alēthinós = the Real/Genuine One (distinct from G227 alēthḗs = merely accurate). The True One in the sense of ultimate ontological reality.

οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ ἀληθινὸς θεὸς καὶ ζωὴ αἰώνιος — "this one is the true God and eternal life." The demonstrative οὗτος points to the nearest antecedent — Jesus Christ, named in the preceding clause. This is the letter's most explicit Christological predication: Jesus Christ is the true God and is eternal life (cf. v.11; Jn 14:6; Jn 20:28).
5.2:
γινώσκομεν — present tense: "we know" — ongoing, repeatable recognition. The verse reverses the verification direction of v.1: there, love for the Father was inferred from love for his children; here, love for the children is verified by love for God and obedience to his commands.

τηρῶμεν τὰς ἐντολὰς αὐτοῦ — G5083 tēréō means to guard or keep watch over — active custody, not casual observance. G1785 entolḗ = authoritative mandate, the language of royal or divine decree rather than general advice.
5.3:
αὕτη γάρ ἐστιν ἡ ἀγάπη τοῦ θεοῦ — subjective genitive: "love for God" consists in this — command-keeping. John defines love as enacted allegiance, not felt warmth. G26 agápē throughout the letter is covenantal, willed, self-giving.

βαρεῖαι οὐκ εἰσίν — G926 barýs means "heavy" (cf. the root behind "burden"). The claim is not that obedience is effortless but that the commands do not crush. John's implicit argument (v.4 supplies the reason): the one born of God has the overcomer's resources.
5.5:
τίς ἐστιν ὁ νικῶν τὸν κόσμον — a rhetorical question with a single, exclusive answer. The present participle ὁ νικῶν ("the one overcoming") sustains the present-tense emphasis of v.4: overcoming is not a past credential but a present reality.

εἰ μὴ ὁ πιστεύων ὅτι Ἰησοῦς ἐστιν ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ θεοῦ — "except the one believing that Jesus is the Son of God." G4100 pisteúō with ὅτι + content: belief that Jesus (the human name) *is* the Son of God (the high confessional title). John insists on the full identification against any Christology that would separate the earthly Jesus from the divine Son.
5.7:
The verse reads simply: ὅτι τρεῖς εἰσιν οἱ μαρτυροῦντες — "For there are three that testify." This is the entire authentic text of 5:7; the sentence continues at 5:8 with the three witnesses.

The expanded reading inserted in some late manuscripts and the Textus Receptus — adding "in heaven: the Father, the Word, and the Holy Spirit, and these three are one" — is known as the Comma Johanneum. It is absent from every Greek manuscript predating the 14th century, from the Vulgate's earliest manuscripts, and from all patristic citations of this passage through the fifth century. Erasmus excluded it; the critical text (UBS5/NA28) omits it. The MKT translation follows the critical text.
5.8:
τὸ πνεῦμα καὶ τὸ ὕδωρ καὶ τὸ αἷμα — the three witnesses each carry the definite article, individuating them. The Spirit testifies in the present (v.6); the water and blood are historical markers — the baptism and crucifixion — that continue to bear testimony because of what they established.

εἰς τὸ ἕν εἰσιν — literally "into the one they are" — they converge toward a single testimony. The phrase does not assert numerical unity of the witnesses but the coherence and agreement of their testimony. The legal principle behind this (Deut 19:15: two or three witnesses establish a matter) is invoked: three witnesses, one verdict, establishing the truth about Jesus Christ.
5.9:
τὴν μαρτυρίαν τῶν ἀνθρώπων λαμβάνομεν — G3141 martyría is legal testimony. G444 ánthrōpos (human being) used generically: we routinely accept human testimony in courts and daily life as establishing facts.

ἡ μαρτυρία τοῦ θεοῦ μείζων ἐστίν — G3187 meízōn, greater: a fortiori argument. If lesser (human) testimony is accepted, how much more the greater (divine). The argument is specific, not general: αὕτη ἐστὶν ἡ μαρτυρία τοῦ θεοῦ ἣν μεμαρτύρηκεν περὶ τοῦ υἱοῦ αὐτοῦ — the perfect memartyréken ("has testified") means this testimony has been given and now stands on record.
5.10:
ὁ πιστεύων εἰς τὸν υἱὸν τοῦ θεοῦ ἔχει τὴν μαρτυρίαν ἐν ἑαυτῷ — G4100 with εἰς + accusative: directed, committed trust "into" the Son, not mere acknowledgment about him. The testimony becomes internal — en heautō, "in himself" — the believer has internalized the divine attestation.

πεποίηκεν αὐτὸν ψεύστην — perfect tense: "has made him a liar" — a standing charge, not a momentary failure. G5583 pseústēs (liar): the one who rejects God's testimony effectively calls God a fabricator. The starkness is deliberate — there is no neutral position between trusting God's witness and impugning it.
5.12:
ὁ ἔχων τὸν υἱὸν ἔχει τὴν ζωήν — G2192 échō ("to have") in both clauses: possession of the Son is possession of life; absence is absence. The antithesis is stark with no middle term. John does not say "the one who knows about the Son" or "the one who admires the Son" — the verb is have.

The second clause adds τοῦ θεοῦ — "the Son of God" — elevating the title to make refusal explicit: to not have the Son is to not have the Son of God, the one in whom the life resides by divine appointment (v.11).
5.13:
ἔγραψα ὑμῖν … ἵνα εἰδῆτε — G1492 oîda (from which eídēte derives) denotes settled, perceptual knowledge — knowledge one already holds, not the process of coming to know (which is G1097 ginṓskō). John's purpose is certainty, not information transfer.

τοῖς πιστεύουσιν εἰς τὸ ὄνομα τοῦ υἱοῦ τοῦ θεοῦ — believers "into the name" — directional trust committed to the name as the full revelation of who Jesus is. The epistle has throughout probed whether the readers' faith is genuine; this closing purpose statement assures rather than threatens.
5.15:
ἐὰν οἴδαμεν ὅτι ἀκούει ἡμῶν — G1492 again: settled certainty that he hears us. ἀκούει is attentive hearing — God listening with regard to what is asked, not mere acoustic reception.

οἴδαμεν ὅτι ἔχομεν τὰ αἰτήματα — G155 aitēmata, the specific requests as formulated. The second oídamen draws the inference: given the first certainty (he hears), the second follows (we have the petitions). The present échomen ("we have") is bold — not "we will eventually receive" but "we hold" — since the hearing God has already engaged the request.
5.17:
πᾶσα ἀδικία ἁμαρτία ἐστίν — G93 adikía (unrighteousness/injustice) is the wider moral category; every instance of it is G266 hamartía. The statement prevents a minimizing reading: nothing in the moral domain is morally neutral.

καὶ ἔστιν ἁμαρτία οὐ πρὸς θάνατον — the qualifying counterclaim: not every sin is the death-kind. V.17 contextualises vv.14–16: the intercessory promise stands for the ordinary domain of Christian struggle; the existence of a sin-unto-death does not nullify the prayer mandate for sins that do not fall in that category.
5.18:
πᾶς ὁ γεγεννημένος ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ οὐχ ἁμαρτάνει — the perfect passive participle "having been born of God" with present "does not sin" — a completed-state and its ongoing implication. This is not a claim of sinless perfection (cf. 1:8–10, 2:1) but of habitual orientation: the defining direction of the life born of God is not sin.

ὁ γεννηθεὶς ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ τηρεῖ αὐτόν — the grammatical subject shifts: ὁ γεννηθεὶς ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ (aorist passive singular, "the one born of God") most naturally refers to the Son — Jesus Christ — as the guardian of believers. The believer does not achieve non-sinning by effort; the Son guards them.

ὁ πονηρός — G4190 with definite article: "the evil one" (personal). G680 háptō in the middle = to take hold of, to grasp — the evil one cannot lay hands on the one under the Son's protection.
5.19:
ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ ἐσμεν — "we are of God" — origin and belonging stated as settled knowledge. This grounds the contrast that follows.

ὁ κόσμος ὅλος ἐν τῷ πονηρῷ κεῖται — G2749 keîtai = "lies." The verb describes passive, inert lying — the world does not struggle against the evil one; it lies in his grip as one who has surrendered. G2889 kósmos here is the world-system ordered apart from God. The contrast with believers (who are "of God" and guarded, v.18) is stark and deliberate.
5.21:
Τεκνία — diminutive of téknon (child), used throughout 1 John with characteristic intimacy: "little children." The pastoral warmth of the address heightens the urgency of the command that follows.

φυλάξατε ἑαυτοὺς ἀπὸ τῶν εἰδώλων — G5442 phylássō aorist imperative: "guard yourselves" — a sharp, decisive act, not a gradual process. The aorist implies urgency: do it now, decisively. G1497 eídōlon = idol, any substitute for the true God. The abruptness is deliberate: after a sustained argument identifying what and who is genuinely real (vv.18–20), the counter is not engagement with idols but active self-protection against them.
